elements.sent-2411-001: Freezing of gait (FOG) is a debilitating symptom of Parkinson’s disease (PD) that impairs mobility and safety.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional detection methods face challenges due to intra and inter-patient variability, and most systems are tested in controlled settings, limiting their real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-003: Addressing these gaps, we present FOGSense, a novel FOG detection system designed for uncontrolled, free-living conditions.
elements.sent-2411-004: It uses Gramian Angular Field (GAF) transformations and federated deep learning to capture temporal and spatial gait patterns missed by traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-005: We evaluated our FOGSense system using a public PD dataset, `tdcsfog'.
elements.sent-2411-006: FOGSense improves accuracy by 10.4\% over a single-axis accelerometer, reduces failure points compared to multi-sensor systems, and demonstrates robustness to missing values.
elements.sent-2411-007: The federated architecture allows personalized model adaptation and efficient smartphone synchronization during off-peak hours, making it effective for long-term monitoring as symptoms evolve.
elements.sent-2411-008: Overall, FOGSense achieves a 22.2\% improvement in F1-score compared to state-of-the-art methods, along with enhanced sensitivity for FOG episode detection.
elements.sent-2411-010: Approximately 90,000 people over the age of 65 are diagnosed with Parkinson's disease (PD) in the U.S.
elements.sent-2411-011: Freezing of Gait (FOG) is one of the most incapacitating symptoms of PD, a neurodegenerative condition that impairs coordination and mobility.
elements.sent-2411-012: The transient inability to start or continue walking, a hallmark of FOG, significantly impairs a patient's mobility and quality of life, increasing the risk of falls, accidents, and major health concerns~\cite{brederecke2023freezing, lichter2021freezing .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, timely detection of FOG and effective interventions can save a PD patient from severe injuries or even death.
elements.sent-2411-014: Timely treatments, including external cues or therapy changes, can help reduce falls, increase mobility, and improve the overall quality of life when FOG is detected early~\cite{ahlrichs2016detecting .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Additionally, real-time detection and ongoing monitoring could keep a doctor well-informed and help modify treatment regimens, which can lessen the frequency and intensity of FOG episodes~\cite{borzi2021prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The integration of wearable technologies with machine learning (ML) represents a major advancement in the continuous monitoring and management of PD symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-017: This convergence enables real-time motion pattern recognition, predictive modeling of FOG episodes, and personalized therapeutic insights.
elements.sent-2411-018: Conventional time series frameworks, however, struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics and variability in patient movement, limiting their effectiveness in processing the multivariate data streams required for real-time FOG prediction and intervention~\cite{pardoel2019wearable,wang2015imaging .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Wearable FOG detection systems also face challenges in sensor reliability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-020: Sensor failures in mobile health monitoring can decrease detection accuracy by up to 65\% when using conventional imputation methods \cite{mamun2022designing .}
elements.sent-2411-021: This vulnerability is especially critical in systems relying on multiple sensors or data axes, where each dependency increases the risk of system failure in real-world deployments~\cite{mamun2022designing .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The Gramian Angular Field (GAF) transformation is a signal processing technique for FOG detection, commonly used with accelerometer and gyroscope data.
elements.sent-2411-023: By converting temporal sequences into 2D representations, GAF improves FOG detection with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)~\cite{elmir2023ecg .}
elements.sent-2411-024: It outperforms wavelet and recurrence plot analysis by preserving local and global patterns while maintaining order~\cite{wang2015imaging .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Mapping data to polar coordinates in the Gramian matrix, GAF captures locomotor perturbations, and its 2D CNN approach extracts richer features than 1D CNNs, boosting FOG detection accuracy~\cite{le2023gaformer,mamani2019gaf .}
elements.sent-2411-026: To further investigate and enhance the efficacy of GAF and CNN for FOG detection, we designed FOGSense, a novel framework for FOG episode detection using only one axis of a single accelerometer sensor.
elements.sent-2411-027: This minimalist approach reduces hardware dependencies and enhances reliability while maintaining strong detection capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-028: By applying GAF transformations with a CNN, FOGSense enables both window-level and episode-level FOG detection.
elements.sent-2411-029: It effectively captures essential gait signatures within brief time windows, allowing for real-time use on resource-limited devices with low computational demand.
elements.sent-2411-030: Additionally, its single-axis design and dynamic weight transfer method improve precision and resilience against sensor failures.
elements.sent-2411-031: Subsequently, we take another step to improve FOGSense by integrating federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-032: Modern smartphones can train models on-device but often strain battery and CPU~\cite{ignatov2019ai,yang2019federated .}
elements.sent-2411-033: FOGSense overcomes this by employing a distributed learning framework tailored for wearable-smartphone ecosystems~\cite{wang2020convergence,khan2020federated .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In this setup, sensor data is transferred during off-peak hours to capture FOG patterns without interrupting regular use~\cite{mazilu2015prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-035: A central server then uses TensorFlow to train models, converts them to TensorFlow Lite for deployment, and adapts these models to both individual and population-wide FOG variations~\cite{mathur2017deepeye,luo2020hfl,li2020federated,rieke2020future .}
elements.sent-2411-036: This approach is optimized for resource-constrained devices~\cite{mathur2017deepeye , with asynchronous updates that balance performance and efficiency~\cite{yang2019federated .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In summary, our contributions are (i) a review of FOG detection methods and challenges, (ii) a FOG detection solution using GAF, CNN, and federated learning with a single accelerometer, (iii) the development and evaluation of the FOGSense system for window-level, episode-level, and federated FOG detection, (iv) performance comparison across channels and with existing methods, and (v) a dynamic weight transfer framework to address channel failure.
elements.sent-2411-038: Recent advancements in FOG detection address the challenges of traditional sensor-based ML methods, with key directions being (1) raw sensor-based deep learning, (2) self-supervised learning (SSL), and (3) federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Raw sensor-based deep learning minimizes manual feature engineering by applying models like 1D-CNNs directly to accelerometer data~\cite{hannink2016sensor,zhao2018hybrid .}
elements.sent-2411-040: These methods reduce preprocessing overhead but require large labeled datasets and often fail to capture the full complexity of sensor signals.
elements.sent-2411-041: Moreover, they struggle with generalizability, particularly when faced with variations in patient populations or sensor placements~\cite{sama2012dyskinesia , limiting their real-world applicability.}
elements.sent-2411-042: SSL techniques aim to overcome labeled data constraints by learning meaningful representations from raw data~\cite{soumma2024self,xia2024prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-043: These methods extract essential features without extensive annotation but face challenges in adapting to diverse sample distributions.
elements.sent-2411-044: Variations in environmental conditions, sensor noise, and individual gait patterns can significantly affect performance~\cite{lonini2018wearable , highlighting the need for robust adaptation mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Federated learning enables collaborative model training across devices while preserving data privacy~\cite{li2020federated,chen2020fedhealth .}
elements.sent-2411-046: This distributed approach is particularly suitable for healthcare but presents challenges in resource-constrained wearables, such as balancing computational efficiency, power consumption, and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-047: Building on these foundations, our work integrates GAF transformation with multichannel CNNs for enhanced feature extraction, surpassing traditional raw sensor-based approaches.
elements.sent-2411-048: Additionally, our federated learning implementation improves model adaptability while preserving privacy and addressing efficiency challenges, making it suitable for resource-constrained real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-049: Developing a reliable FOG detection model is challenging due to the complexity of PD and symptom variability across patients.
elements.sent-2411-050: Our hierarchical system architecture, illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:federated_system_design , addresses these challenges while accommodating the constraints of wearable devices.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Wearables continuously collect acceleration data, which is transferred to paired smartphones at designated intervals.
elements.sent-2411-052: A central federated server aggregates this data during off-peak hours, applies GAF transformations, and executes model training.
elements.sent-2411-053: The processed data are stored in a time-series database for comprehensive health monitoring and temporal analysis.
elements.sent-2411-054: Updated model weights are saved in an in-memory database for efficient synchronization with smartphones, which then update wearables.
elements.sent-2411-055: This design ensures efficient data handling, robust model distribution, and reliable FOG monitoring.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth, trim={0 150 0 50}, clip]{fig/Whole_system_final.pdf} \caption{System architecture illustrating the federated learning workflow: local models are trained and uploaded by devices, then aggregated into a global model, which is distributed back to each device.} \label{fig:Whole Design}
elements.sent-2411-056: We used the publicly available PD dataset ~\cite{tlvmc-parkinsons-freezing-gait-prediction from the TLVMC FOG prediction competition.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The dataset has 62 subjects diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease, specifically, focusing on FOG episodes.
elements.sent-2411-058: The analysis concentrated on the `tdcsfog' dataset.
elements.sent-2411-059: Data were collected via a lower-back triaxial accelerometer at 128 Hz, capturing vertical (AccV), mediolateral (AccML), and anteroposterior (AccAP) movements, critical for identifying directional mobility loss during FOG events.
elements.sent-2411-060: Rich temporal annotations, including start hesitations, turns, and walking, enabled precise FOG analysis.
elements.sent-2411-061: Using DHWT the FOG window counts rose from 1,096,265 to 1,994,705, yielding an 82\% increase.
elements.figure-2411-003: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth,trim={0 60 0 20}, clip]{fig/berore_after_bhtw.pdf} \begin{center} (a) Before DHWT (b) After DHWT \end{center} \caption{Class distribution normal and FOG events before (a) and after (b) applying Differential Hopping Windowing Technique (DHWT).} \label{fig:fog_event_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-062: The dataset, originally collected at 128Hz, was downsampled to 64Hz to reduce computational complexity while preserving signal characteristics~\cite{soumma2024self .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The `tdcsfog' data's three event types were consolidated into a single column, labeling FOG as 1 and no-FOG as 0.
elements.sent-2411-064: A critical preprocessing step involved defining time windows for analysis, which we set at 4-second intervals to effectively capture essential gait features for FOG detection~\cite{naghavi2019prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-065: To address class imbalance effectively, we incorporated the Differential Hopping Windowing Technique (DHWT)\cite{soumma2024self .}
elements.sent-2411-066: This technique applied varying overlap sizes: no overlap (0\%) for no-FOG windows and an increased 50\% overlap for FOG windows.
elements.sent-2411-067: By using this method, we enhanced the representation of FOG events, mitigating class imbalance and enriching the model's training dataset without additional preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-068: Fig \ref{fig:fog_event_distribution illustrates the results achieved with this approach, showing a more balanced distribution of FOG and no-FOG instances.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We filtered out impure windows where no-FOG events (class 0) predominated, designating windows where FOG events (class 1) were in the majority as pure FOG windows.
elements.sent-2411-070: For improved data consistency and distribution, we subtracted the mean value from each window ($X_{i,j ^{\prime = X_{i,j - \mu_{X_i}$), ensuring normalization across samples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Our methodology employs Conv2D CNN for FOG episode detection.
elements.sent-2411-072: To leverage CNNs' proven capabilities in image processing, we transformed our temporal acceleration data into image-based representations using the GAF method.
elements.sent-2411-073: This transformation encodes temporal dependencies into a higher-dimensional space, allowing the CNN architecture to potentially capture complex patterns that might be less apparent in the original time series format.
elements.sent-2411-074: The GAF transformation effectively converts 1D time series data into 2D images suitable for CNN-based analysis while preserving temporal and dynamic structures~\cite{yokkampon2022autoencoder .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The transformation process consists of two key steps: normalization and polar coordinate mapping.
elements.sent-2411-076: First, the time series data is normalized to the range $[-1,1]$ through the following transformations: \begin{equation}x'_i = \frac{x_i - \min(X)}{\max(X) - \min(X)} \theta_i = \cos^{-1}(x'_i) \label{eq:angle} [6pt] x''_i = 2x'_i - 1 r_i = \frac{t_i}{N} \label{eq:radius}\end{equation} where $t_i$ represents the time stamp and $N$ denotes the total number of points.
elements.figure-2411-004: [H] \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth, trim={20 170 10 60}, clip]{fig/1dtotime.pdf} \begin{center} (a) Time series (b) Polar coordinates (c) GAF image \end{center} \caption{GAF transformation of accelerometer signals.} \label{fig:Transform_image}
elements.sent-2411-077: The next stage in the GAF process involves creating the Gram matrix, which captures temporal correlations between different time points using trigonometric functions.
elements.sent-2411-078: Specifically, for the Gramian Angular Summation Field (GASF), we compute: \begin{equation}\text{GASF}(i,j) = \cos(\theta_i + \theta_j) \label{eq:gasf}\end{equation} For a time series of $m$ data points, the resulting GASF matrix takes the form: \begin{equation}\text{GAF} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_1) \cdots \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_m) \vdots \ddots \vdots \cos(\theta_m + \theta_1) \cdots \cos(\theta_m + \theta_m) \end{bmatrix} \label{eq:gaf_matrix}\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth,trim={0 0 10 10}, clip]{fig/Last_comparison.pdf} \begin{center} (a) No-FOG image (b) FOG image (c) Differences \end{center} \caption{GAF-transformed images for (a) No-FOG, (b) FOG conditions, and (c) differences, with significantly different regions highlighted with red boxes.} \label{fig:Diffrence_image}
elements.sent-2411-079: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:Diffrence_image , the GAF transformation creates distinct visual patterns for normal (Fig.~\ref{fig:Diffrence_image a) and FOG (Fig.~\ref{fig:Diffrence_image b) conditions, enabling image-based deep learning applications.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The annotated difference, based on pixel-wise absolute differences between GAF-transformed images, highlights areas with significant pattern variations, capturing subtle gait changes.
elements.sent-2411-081: The top 15 regions with the highest difference values are emphasized, showcasing where the FOG pattern deviates most from no-FOG.
elements.sent-2411-082: This approach effectively captures temporal patterns in sequential data, making it ideal for medical signal processing and gait analysis \cite{buz2020novel .}
elements.sent-2411-083: We used subject-based stratified partitioning to prevent data leakage and ensure robust evaluation~\cite{saeb2017scalable .}
elements.sent-2411-084: The dataset was split into training (70\%), validation (10\%), and testing (20\%) sets using randomly shuffled subject identifiers and it preserved FOG/no-FOG proportions and temporal integrity~\cite{hammerla2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-085: This procedure is repeated for each group within the dataset, conducted three times, and the results are averaged to ensure reliability and robustness in our findings.
elements.sent-2411-086: Our FOG detection system employs a sophisticated multichannel 2D Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture designed to maximize performance through spatial relationship analysis across multiple sensor modalities.
elements.sent-2411-087: The architecture processes data from three distinct accelerometer axes—Vertical (AccV), Mediolateral (AccML), and Anteroposterior (AccAP)—by transforming them into GAF images and processing them through dedicated CNN branches.
elements.sent-2411-088: This approach enables the system to leverage complementary information from different sensor perspectives, significantly enhancing its FOG detection capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-089: The proposed architecture, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:cnn_image , consists of three parallel CNN branches, each dedicated to processing GAF images from a specific accelerometer axis.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Each branch processes input data with dimensions (64, 64, 3) through three consecutive convolutional blocks of increasing complexity.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Dense layers after feature concatenation.} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.5} \begin{tabular}{lccc} Layer Units Regularization Dropout First Dense 128 L2 ($\lambda=0.001$) 40\% Second Dense 64 None 20\% Output Dense 2 None None \end{tabular} \label{tab:dense_layers}
elements.sent-2411-091: The first block utilizes 32 filters with 20\% dropout, while the second and third blocks employ 64 and 128 filters respectively, incorporating L2 regularization ($\lambda=0.001$) and increasing dropout rates (20\% and 40\%).
elements.sent-2411-092: Each convolutional block integrates batch normalization and max pooling operations to enhance feature learning and reduce spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-093: A global average pooling layer follows the convolutional blocks, effectively reducing parameters while capturing essential global features from each branch.
elements.sent-2411-094: Features extracted from individual branches are concatenated to create a unified representation, which then passes through two dense layers for final classification.
elements.sent-2411-095: The first dense layer comprises 128 neurons with ReLU activation, L2 regularization ($\lambda=0.001$), and 40\% dropout, followed by a second dense layer of 64 neurons with ReLU activation and 20\% dropout.
elements.sent-2411-096: The final classification is performed by a softmax layer with two neurons corresponding to FOG and no-FOG classes.
elements.sent-2411-097: To ensure robust performance and prevent overfitting, the model incorporates multiple regularization techniques Adam optimizer with the categorical cross-entropy loss function.
elements.sent-2411-098: The backbone architecture remained unchanged during channel configuration analysis, ensuring performance differences stemmed solely from input channel variations.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, we also proposed a dynamic weight transfer mechanism to handle the failure of any sensor channel.
elements.sent-2411-100: As the architecture is the same for each channel, when a channel fails, the system can load the weights for a different functional channel and predict with that channel.
elements.sent-2411-101: For example, if the channel AccV fails at any time and the channel AccAP is functional, the FOGSense system will load the weights trained with AccAP and make the prediction.
elements.sent-2411-103: FOGSense was evaluated using standard binary classification metrics.
elements.sent-2411-104: Based on the confusion matrix elements—true positives (TP), false positives (FP), false negatives (FN), and true negatives (TN)—we computed the following metrics: sensitivity, precision, accuracy, and F1-score.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Inference with Dynamic Weight Transfer} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Training and testing before deployment: \State Train $N$ models of identical architecture separately for $N$ channels and evaluate them on the test dataset. \State Sort the $N$ models and channels in descending order of their test F-1 scores as: \Statex $\text{Models} = (\text{Model}_1, \text{Model}_2, \dots, \text{Model}_N)$ \Statex $\text{Channels} = (\text{Channel}_1, \text{Channel}_2, \dots, \text{Channel}_N)$ \State Save the weights of all models. \State Inference after deployment: \State Find the smallest $i$ such that $\text{Channel}_i$ is functional in new data. \State Load weights of $\text{Model}_i$. \State Predict using the $i$-th channel's readings in the new data. \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:dynamic_weight_transfer}
elements.sent-2411-105: Our experiments excluded conventional data augmentation to preserve GAF signal integrity.
elements.sent-2411-106: We evaluated window-level and temporal FOG detection, achieving promising results.
elements.sent-2411-107: To enhance clinical applicability, we used federated learning for continuous model adaptation, preserving privacy and enabling model refinement across edge devices.
elements.figure-2411-008: \caption{FOGSense's performance for three different detection types using all three channels. DFW: Detected FOG Windows, DFE: Detected FOG Episodes.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{4pt} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.8} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lccc} Detection Type Configuration Accuracy (\%) F1 (\%) Window-level (DFW) All Channels 87.23 90.31 Episode-level (DFE) All Channels 87.23 90.86 Federated Learning All Channels 86.98 90.47 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:performance_summary}
elements.sent-2411-108: We conducted a systematic evaluation of input channel configurations to optimize our FOG detection framework.
elements.sent-2411-109: Initially utilizing all three accelerometer axes (anterior-posterior, mediolateral, and vertical), we investigated the impact of channel reduction on model performance.
elements.sent-2411-110: The analysis focused on quantifying the trade-off between computational efficiency and FOG detection accuracy across different channel combinations to determine the optimal configuration.
elements.sent-2411-111: Here, each model was trained for 60 epochs.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Impact of selection of AccV (Vertical), AccML (Mediolateral), and AccAP (Anteroposterior) axes.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.2pt} 0.78{ \begin{tabular}{cccccccc} \multirow{2}{*}{Channels} \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell{Input Combination}} \multicolumn{2}{c}{Duration (s/epoch)} \multirow{2}{*}{ACC} \multirow{2}{*}{F1} \multirow{2}{*}{PRE} \multirow{2}{*}{SEN} \cmidrule(l){3-4} Train Test 3 AccAP-AccML-AccV 63.0 12.9 87.2 90.3 90.3 91.0 \multirow{3}{*}{ 2} AccAP-AccML 32.1 15.4 93.2 87.7 93.2 93.2 AccML-AccV 31.8 19.0 95.3 90.8 95.3 95.3 AccAP-AccV 31.7 13.3 94.5 90.5 94.5 94.5 \multirow{3}{*}{ 1} AccAP 11.3 11.0 93.2 93.0 93.2 93.2 AccML 17.7 19.0 89.2 83.0 89.2 89.2 AccV 11.3 11.0 96.3 96.3 95.8 96.3 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:something-else}
elements.sent-2411-112: Table~\ref{tab:something-else reveals several key insights regarding channel configuration impact on model performance.}
elements.sent-2411-113: The baseline three-channel configuration (AccAP-AccML-AccV) achieved 87.23\% accuracy and 90.31\% F1-score, requiring 63 seconds per training epoch.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, reducing to two channels generally improved performance while halving the training time to approximately 32 seconds per epoch.
elements.sent-2411-115: Among dual-channel configurations, AccML-AccV demonstrated superior performance with 95.30\% accuracy and 90.75\% F1-score.
elements.sent-2411-116: Notably, single-channel configurations further reduced training time to approximately 11 seconds per epoch while maintaining or improving performance.
elements.sent-2411-117: The AccV channel alone achieved the highest overall accuracy of 96.25\% and F1-score of 96.27\%, surpassing both three-channel and two-channel configurations.
elements.sent-2411-118: In contrast, AccML showed a relatively lower performance (89.24\% accuracy), while AccAP maintained a robust performance (93.24\% accuracy).
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth, trim={85 0 100 0}, clip]{fig/Resultt.pdf} \caption{Comparison of channel configurations in the FOGSense system shows that the vertical axis (AccV) obtains a better F1 score than other channel choices.} \label{fig:channel_comparison_radar}
elements.sent-2411-119: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:channel_comparison_radar , the single AccV channel configuration (highlighted in red) emerges as the optimal choice, offering superior performance metrics while minimizing computational overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-120: This finding suggests that AccV alone captures sufficient information for effective FOG detection, making it particularly suitable for resource-constrained applications.
elements.sent-2411-121: Additionally, this configuration is resilient to missing values, as it continues to perform effectively even when data from other axes is unavailable, achieving better results using only the AccV channel.
elements.sent-2411-122: We present a comparative analysis of FOGSense and the other state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in Table~\ref{tab:performance-compare .}
elements.sent-2411-123: Our FOGSense method achieves an F1 score of 0.96 on the `tdcsfog' dataset, whereas the closest competitor LIFT-PD achieved an F1 score of 0.79.
elements.sent-2411-124: Moreover, unlike the other methods, the FOGSense maintains a score of at least 0.90 in all four metrics.
elements.sent-2411-125: Additionally, FOGSense's use of the vertical axis (AccV) channel selection not only enhances detection accuracy but also provides a more efficient approach to gait monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-126: These results underscore FOGSense's potential as a powerful and dependable tool for real-time gait analysis.
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{FOGSense vs. state-of-the-art on tdcsfog. The best values for each metric are highlighted in bold, while the second-best values are underlined.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.6} 0.92{ \begin{tabular}{c|*{3}{c} c} Study DFE SEN (DFW) PRE F1 One Class Classifier~\cite{naghavi2021towards} 90.8\% 71.6\% 0.86 0.77 Semi-Supervised Model~\cite{mikos2017real} -- 72.3\% -- -- Multi-head CNN~\cite{10.1016/j.artmed.2022.102459} 97.3\% 94.6\% 0.56 0.68 LIFT-PD 88\% {84\%} {0.74} 0.79 FOGSense -- 96.3\% 0.958 0.963 \end{tabular}} \label{tab:performance-compare}
elements.sent-2411-127: In this paper, we introduced a novel approach to detect FOG by integrating GAF-transformed accelerometer data with a multichannel CNN architecture and a decentralized federated learning pipeline.
elements.sent-2411-128: Our experiments demonstrated not only high detection accuracy but also highlighted the practicality of our system for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-129: A key finding is that a single-channel configuration using only the vertical acceleration component (AccV) achieved impressive performance—96.25\% accuracy and 96.27\% F1-score—while significantly reducing computational demands compared to multi-channel configurations.
elements.sent-2411-130: This result challenges the traditional view that multiple sensor channels are essential for accurate FOG detection.
elements.sent-2411-131: To address continuous monitoring constraints, we developed a distributed framework that synchronizes data between wearable devices and smartphones during off-peak hours, balancing the demands of monitoring, battery life, and computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-132: Although the GAF transformation was effective in our approach, exploring alternative time series representations such as Recurrence Plots and Markov Transition Fields may yield further insights.
elements.sent-2411-133: Additionally, while our distributed framework shows promise in simulations, real-world validation on multiple devices is a critical next step.
elements.sent-2411-134: Investigating different weight calculation methods beyond weighted averaging could enhance the learning dynamics in federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-135: Testing the model across diverse patient populations and datasets would further strengthen its generalizability, and studying patient data mixing strategies may provide insights into managing individual gait variations effectively.
elements.sent-2411-136: Accurate detection of freezing of gait (FOG) can significantly enhance early intervention strategies, improve mobility support, and ultimately contribute to a better quality of life for individuals with Parkinson’s disease.
elements.sent-2411-137: Our FOGSense system uses Gramian Angular Field (GAF) imaging, convolutional neural networks (CNN), and federated learning to ensure accurate and resource-efficient FOG detection in an uncontrolled environment.
elements.sent-2411-138: Moreover, FOGSense is robust to sensor failure as it needs only one channel to make an accurate prediction and it can delegate the prediction task to a different single-channel model by dynamic weight transfer in case of a channel failure.
elements.sent-2411-139: Our work establishes a foundation for efficient, practical, and reliable FOG detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-140: We provide new insights into lightweight, single-channel solutions that outperform state-of-the-art multi-channel solutions.
elements.sent-2411-141: We also identified future research directions that could advance the field, from alternative data representations to federated learning enhancements and real-world validations.
elements.sent-2411-142: This study highlights the potential of FOGSense and opens avenues for refining FOG detection systems to meet clinical and practical demands more effectively.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Freezing of gait (FOG) is a debilitating symptom of Parkinson’s disease that impairs mobility and safety by increasing the risk of falls.
elements.sent-2411-144: An effective FOG detection system must be accurate, real-time, and deployable in free-living environments to enable timely interventions.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, existing detection methods face challenges due to (1) intra- and inter-patient variability, (2) subject-specific training, (3) using multiple sensors in FOG dominant locations (e.g., ankles) leading to high failure points, (4) centralized, non-adaptive learning frameworks that sacrifice patient privacy and prevent collaborative model refinement across populations and disease progression, and (5) most systems are tested in controlled settings, limiting their real-world applicability for continuous in-home monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-146: Addressing these gaps, we present FOGSense, a real-world deployable FOG detection system designed for uncontrolled, free-living conditions using only a single sensor.
elements.sent-2411-147: FOGSense uses Gramian Angular Field (GAF) transformations and privacy-preserving federated deep learning to capture temporal and spatial gait patterns missed by traditional methods with a low false positive rate.
elements.sent-2411-148: We evaluated our system using a public Parkinson's dataset collected in a free-living environment.
elements.sent-2411-149: Overall, FOGSense achieved a 22.2\% improvement in F1-score and a 74.53\% reduction in false positive rate compared to state-of-the-art methods, along with enhanced sensitivity for FOG episode detection, empowering preventive care and long-term symptom management as Parkinson’s progresses.
elements.sent-2411-150: Freezing of Gait (FOG) is one of the most incapacitating symptoms of Parkinson's disease (PD), a neurodegenerative condition that impairs coordination and mobility.
elements.sent-2411-151: The integration of wearables with machine learning (ML) represents a major advancement in the continuous monitoring and management of PD symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-152: However, the effectiveness of ML-driven FOG detection depends heavily on sensor placement, reliability, and adaptability, as capturing nuanced gait dynamics remains a challenge~\cite{tao2012gait .}
elements.sent-2411-153: While CyclePro~\cite{8616844 demonstrates the utility of wearable sensors for continuous gait cycle detection, especially in noisy environments, it also highlights the necessity for platform-independent solutions that do not require predefined parameters, which could greatly benefit FOG detection systems.}
elements.sent-2411-154: SmartSock~\cite{fallahzadeh2016smartsock offers a robust, wearable platform for continuous monitoring, using machine learning and signal processing techniques to provide real-time, context-aware assessments of ankle edema.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Multi-sensor systems, while providing richer data, introduce critical reliability concerns.
elements.sent-2411-156: Wearable FOG detection solutions that rely on multiple sensors at FoG-dominant locations (e.g., ankles~\cite{koltermann2024gait or shoes~\cite{park2024detection ) are susceptible to sensor failures, misalignment, and increased system complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Sensor failures can decrease detection accuracy by up to 65\%~\cite{mamun2022designing , especially in systems that rely on multiple channels, where each dependency increases the risk of system failure in real-world deployments.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Despite the advantages of wearables, FOG detection remains challenging due to the complex, non-linear nature of gait disturbances~\cite{liu2024adaptive .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Conventional time series frameworks struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics and variability in gait movement, limiting their effectiveness in processing the multivariate data streams required for real-time FOG detection and intervention~\cite{pardoel2019wearable,Soumma_Mamun_Ghasemzadeh_2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: To address this, we leverage Gramian Angular Field (GAF) transformations, a signal processing technique that converts temporal sequences into 2D representations and improves FOG detection~\cite{elmir2023ecg .}
elements.sent-2411-161: It outperforms wavelet and recurrence plot analysis by preserving local and global patterns while maintaining order~\cite{wang2015imaging , boosting FOG detection accuracy~\cite{le2023gaformer .}}
elements.sent-2411-162: To further investigate and enhance the efficacy of GAF and Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for FOG detection, we designed FOGSense, a novel framework for FOG episode detection using only one axis of a single accelerometer sensor with a low false positive rate.
elements.sent-2411-163: Modern smartphones can train models on-device but often strain battery and CPU~\cite{ignatov2019ai,9928465 .}
elements.sent-2411-164: This setup transfers sensor data during off-peak hours to capture FOG patterns without interrupting regular use~\cite{mazilu2015prediction .}
elements.sent-2411-165: A central server then uses TensorFlow to train models, converts them to TensorFlow Lite for deployment, and adapts these models to both individual and population-wide FOG variations~\cite{luo2020hfl,li2020federated,rieke2020future .}
elements.sent-2411-166: To achieve this, we explore the research question: How can we reduce false positive (FP) intervention rates while maintaining high FOG detection accuracy, ensuring the model adapts to different patients over time without compromising privacy? In summary, our contributions are (i) a real-world deployable, privacy-preserving FOG detection system using a single lower-back accelerometer, reducing multi-sensor failure risks, (ii) Integration of GAF transformations and federated learning, enhancing gait pattern recognition with lower FP rate (iii) the development and evaluation of the FOGSense system for window-level, episode-level, and federated FOG detection, (iv) performance comparison across channels and with existing methods, and (v) a dynamic weight transfer framework to address channel failure.
elements.sent-2411-167: Raw sensor-based deep learning minimizes manual feature engineering by applying models like 1D-CNNs directly to accelerometer data~\cite{zhao2018hybrid .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Recent works like DGAD ~\cite{naghavi2021towards , FoG-Finder~\cite{koltermann2023fog and Gait-Guard~\cite{koltermann2024gait use two ankle-worn IMUs and CNN-based models for real-time FoG detection.}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: DGAD applies a Butterworth bandpass filter (0.5-8Hz) to IMU data before using a single-headed CNN for classification.
elements.sent-2411-170: While some of these systems achieve strong detection performance, they rely on more than one sensor, increasing hardware complexity and failure risks, and are tested in a controlled setting.
elements.sent-2411-171: A central federated server aggregates this data during off-peak hours, applies GAF transformations, and executes model training as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:fogsense_Design .}
elements.sent-2411-172: We trained a multi-channel CNN model across five federated clients, each simulating different data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-173: Clients were assigned different combinations of gait data, allowing the system to learn personalized gait variations without centralizing sensitive patient data.
elements.sent-2411-174: We used a weighted averaging strategy (FedAvg) to aggregate client updates~\cite{beaussart2021waffle , ensuring a balanced contribution to the global model while preserving model stability.}
elements.sent-2411-175: Each client trained on local epochs before synchronizing with the central server, which converted the models to TensorFlow Lite for efficient deployment on resource-constrained devices.
elements.sent-2411-176: This approach enables continuous, personalized adaptation of FOG detection while maintaining low communication overhead and strong privacy guarantees.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.94\linewidth, trim={0 150 0 50}, clip]{fig/Whole_system_final.pdf} \caption{System architecture illustrating the federated learning workflow: local models are trained and uploaded by devices, then aggregated into a global model, which is distributed back to each device.} \label{fig:fogsense_Design}
elements.sent-2411-177: The dataset has 62 subjects diagnosed with Parkinson’s disease, specifically focusing on FOG episodes.
elements.sent-2411-178: Using the ``Differential Hopping Windowing Technique'' (DHWT)~\cite{soumma2024self , the FOG window counts rose from 1,096,265 to 1,994,705, yielding an 82\% increase.}
elements.figure-2411-013: [H] \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth,trim={0 60 0 20}, clip]{fig/berore_after_bhtw.pdf} \begin{center} (a) Before DHWT (b) After DHWT \end{center} \caption{Class distribution normal and FOG events before (a) and after (b) applying (DHWT) to the training set.} \label{fig:fog_event_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-179: We used subject-based stratified partitioning to prevent data leakage and ensure robust evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-180: To address class imbalance effectively, we used DHWT~\cite{soumma2024self on our training set.}
elements.sent-2411-181: Fig.~\ref{fig:fog_event_distribution illustrates the results achieved with this approach, showing a more balanced distribution of FOG and no-FOG instances.}
elements.sent-2411-182: FOGSense uses a multichannel 2D-CNN architecture designed to maximize performance through spatial relationship analysis across multiple sensor modalities.
elements.sent-2411-183: The model processes data from three distinct accelerometer axes (vertical, mediolateral, and anteroposterior) by transforming them into GAF images and processing them through dedicated CNN branches.
elements.sent-2411-184: All experiments, including pre-processing, model training, and federated learning, were conducted using Python 3.8, TensorFlow 2.2, and one NVIDIA A100 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-185: The proposed architecture (Fig.~\ref{fig:cnn_image ) consists of three parallel CNN branches, each processing GAF images from a specific accelerometer axis.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Each branch processes input data with dimensions (64, 64, 3) through three consecutive convolutional blocks with increasing complexity - starting with 32 filters (20\% dropout), followed by 64 and 128 filters, incorporating L2 regularization ($\lambda=0.001$) and dropout rates of 20\% and 40\%.
elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{Dense layers after feature concatenation.} \begin{tabular}{lccc} Layer Units Regularization Dropout First Dense 128 L2 ($\lambda=0.001$) 40\% Second Dense 64 None 20\% Output Dense 2 None None \end{tabular} \label{tab:dense_layers}
elements.sent-2411-187: Batch normalization and max pooling enhance feature extraction while reducing spatial dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-188: A global average pooling layer further compresses parameters before features from all branches are concatenated.
elements.sent-2411-189: The final classifier consists of two dense layers (128 and 64 neurons with ReLU activation, dropout (40\%, 20\%), and L2 regularization) followed by a softmax layer for FOG classification.
elements.sent-2411-190: Adam optimizer and categorical cross-entropy loss are used to improve generalization.
elements.sent-2411-191: The backbone architecture remained unchanged during channel configuration analysis to ensure consistent performance evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-192: Based on the confusion matrix elements—true positives (TP), false positives (FP), false negatives (FN), and true negatives (TN)—we computed the following metrics: sensitivity, precision, accuracy, F1-score, and false positive rate (FPR).
elements.sent-2411-193: FPR is crucial in FOG detection as it quantifies incorrect intervention triggers, ensuring the system minimizes unnecessary alerts; otherwise, treatment efficacy will decrease.
elements.sent-2411-194: To ensure the integrity of GAF-transformed signals, we did not apply conventional data augmentation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-195: We evaluated FOGSense for both window-level and episode-level detection, achieving consistently high performance.
elements.sent-2411-196: Window-level detection refers to independently classifying each window as FOG or non-FOG and episode-level detection merges contiguous windows to form a single FOG event.
elements.sent-2411-197: To improve clinical applicability, we implemented federated learning, allowing continuous model adaptation while preserving privacy.
elements.sent-2411-198: Table~\ref{tab:performance_summary shows that FOGSense achieves high accuracy across all detection levels, with F1-scores above 90\% and a low false positive rate (3.2-3.8\%).}
elements.sent-2411-199: Episode-level detection performs best, minimizing false alarms.

DFW: Detected FOG Windows, DFE: Detected FOG Episodes, FPR: False Positive Rate.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{3.9pt} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.8} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lcccc} Detection Type Configuration Accuracy (\%) F1 (\%) FPR (\%) Window-level (DFW) All Channels 87.23 90.31 3.8 Episode-level (DFE) All Channels 87.23 90.86 3.2 Federated Learning All Channels 86.98 90.47 3.5 \end{tabular} } \label{tab:performance_summary}

elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth, trim={85 0 100 0}, clip]{fig/Resultt.pdf} \caption{Comparison of channel configurations in the FOGSense system shows that the vertical axis (AccV) obtains a better F1 score than other channel choices.} \label{fig:channel_comparison_radar}
elements.sent-2411-200: Our FOGSense method achieves an F1 score of 0.96 on the `tdcsfog' dataset, whereas the closest competitor, LIFT-PD achieved an F1 score of 0.79.
elements.sent-2411-201: Moreover, unlike the other methods, the FOGSense maintains a score of at least 0.90 in all four metrics, with an average 74.5\% reduction in FPR.
elements.figure-2411-018: \caption{FOGSense vs. state-of-the-art on tdcsfog. The best values are highlighted in bold, while the second-best values are underlined.} \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2.5pt} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.9} 1{ \begin{tabular}{c|*{3}{c} cc} Study DFE SEN (DFW) PRE F1 FPR One Class Classifier~\cite{naghavi2021towards} 90.8\% 71.6\% 0.86 0.77 12.4\% Semi-Supervised Model~\cite{mikos2017real} -- 72.3\% -- -- 11.8\% Multi-head CNN~\cite{10.1016/j.artmed.2022.102459} 97.3\% 94.6\% 0.56 0.68 20.5\% LIFT-PD~\cite{soumma2024self} 88\% {84\%} {0.74} 0.79 9.6\% FOGSense -- 96.3\% 0.958 0.963 3.2\% \end{tabular}} \label{tab:performance-compare}
elements.sent-2411-202: Our experiments demonstrated not only high detection accuracy with a low false positive intervention rate but also highlighted the practicality of our system for in-home real-time monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-203: This result challenges the traditional view that multiple sensor channels are essential for accurate FOG detection while ensuring a balance between high accuracy and minimal false positives, preventing unnecessary interventions that could reduce treatment efficacy over time.
elements.sent-2411-204: Additionally, while our distributed framework shows promise in simulations, real-world validation on multiple edge devices is a critical next step.
elements.sent-2411-205: Our FOGSense system uses Gramian Angular Field (GAF) imaging, CNN, and federated learning to ensure accurate and resource-efficient FOG detection in an uncontrolled environment.
elements.sent-2411-206: Moreover, FOGSense maintains reliability even with sensor failure, as it needs only one channel to make an accurate prediction and it can delegate the prediction task to a different single-channel model by dynamic weight transfer in case of a channel failure.
elements.sent-2411-207: We provide new insights into lightweight, single-channel solutions that outperform multi-channel approaches, achieving a 22.2\% improvement in F1-score and a 74.5\% reduction in false positive rate (FPR), minimizing unnecessary interventions that could diminish their therapeutic impact over time.
elements.sent-2411-208: By reducing false positives, FOGSense ensures that patients receive interventions only when needed, preventing desensitization to stimuli and maintaining long-term clinical effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-209: This study highlights the potential of FOGSense and opens avenues for refining real-time FOG detection systems to meet clinical and practical demands more effectively.. \bibliographystyle{ieeetr.}
